real_questions.text: For each fund finance structure you use — rated note feeders, continuation vehicles, CFOs, NAV loans, subscription facilities — defend the LTV and advance-rate discipline.

strong_answer_markers.text: Maps each private credit risk the agency raises to the specific mitigant in the structure — secured position, equity cushion, coverage ratio — rather than disputing that the risk exists.

agency_intel: Agency meetings — annual reviews especially — are update meetings: expect the agenda to be driven by recent events and the agencies' latest pressure points, with questions of the form "how is [recent event] impacting your portfolio/business, and how are you addressing it?" Come with a prepared current-events readout, not just factor fundamentals.

agency_intel.text: Fund finance structural risks agencies raise, by structure: rated note feeders — LTVs, advance rates, portfolio concentration. Continuation vehicle financing — LTV, advance rates, concentration, volatile/risky underlying assets, cash sweep holidays. CFOs — volatile/risky underlying assets increasing. NAV loans — loan-to-value and advance rates are the biggest risk. Subscription facilities — tranching into subclasses is an increasing risk, along with LTVs. Issuers active in any of these should expect structure-specific questions.

The private credit risk probe list — treat as the checklist of expected questions for any private credit lender or investor: outsized growth; lack of through-the-cycle track record; deterioration in lending terms; rising borrower credit risk; higher loan concentration; competition pressuring spreads; borrower maturity walls; climbing borrower-level leverage; less transparency and regulation versus bank lending; banks lending to NBFIs creating second-order exposure; and insurers among the largest private credit investors, creating policyholder downside. Have a prepared answer for each.

The mitigant answer key agencies look for against the private credit risk list: secured lending with meaningful equity cushions; alignment of interests; declining base rates supporting borrower debt service; limited liquidity transformation; solid asset coverage and low leverage; small market size limiting systemic impact; banks' private credit exposure low relative to equity; strong capital buffers; diversification; strong underwriting; and regulatory oversight matched with limited redemption risk.

agency_intel: Fitch's 2026 macro watch items for the NBFI space: evolution of the US–Iran conflict and its impact on global growth, asset quality, and market sentiment; monetary policy and inflation expectations (lender revenue, asset quality, funding costs/availability, credit demand); and transaction activity (M&A, IPOs) driving valuations, realizations, and fee generation. Fitch has turned more negative on NBFIs overall: growth revised down post US–Iran war, most expected rate cuts have not materialized, inflation risks up. Latin American NBFIs carry added election-cycle political uncertainty.
